summary: A biographical overview of Andrew Carnegie's life, detailing his rise from a poor Scottish immigrant to a wealthy industrialist, and his subsequent dedication to philanthropy. It highlights his core philosophies on wealth distribution, the importance of public libraries for self-education, and his ultimate pursuit of world peace.

principles.statement: The wealthy have a moral obligation to give away their fortunes during their lifetimes to benefit society.
principles.rationale: The rich should act as trustees of their wealth, living without extravagance and using their riches to promote the welfare and happiness of others rather than hoarding it until death.
principles.source_quote: The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.

principles.statement: Funding public libraries is the most productive use of philanthropic money.
principles.rationale: Providing access to books allows ambitious individuals to educate themselves and succeed, while also helping immigrants acquire cultural knowledge.
principles.source_quote: It was from my own early experience that I decided there was no use to which money could be applied so productive of good to boys and girls who have good within them and ability and ambition to develop it, as the founding of a public library in a community.

principles.statement: Philanthropic trusts must remain flexible to adapt to changing times.
principles.rationale: The world changes inevitably, so binding trustees to rigid, perpetual paths is unwise; they must be allowed to use their own judgment.
principles.source_quote: Conditions upon the earth inevitably change, hence no wise man will bind Trustees forever to certain paths, causes or institutions…. They shall best conform to my wishes by using their own judgment.

principles.statement: The true noble motive in life is to improve the world.
principles.rationale: Leaving the world better than you found it is the highest calling a person can have.
principles.source_quote: To try to make the world in some way better than you found it is to have a noble motive in life.

frameworks.name: The Gospel of Wealth
frameworks.when_to_apply: When managing and distributing vast personal wealth.
steps: Provide moderately for your family and dependents.
Act as a trustee for your remaining wealth, distributing it during your lifetime to promote the welfare and happiness of others.

mental_models.name: The Three-Legged Stool of Industry
mental_models.description: A metaphor viewing the industrial world as an alliance of three equal, interdependent forces: labor, capital, and ability. If any one leg is missing or unequal, the enterprise cannot stand.
mental_models.example: Carnegie used this model to explain that workers (labor), money (capital), and management/skill (ability) are all equally necessary to move the industrial world.

heuristics: Do not bind trustees forever to certain paths; allow them to use their own judgment as conditions change.

signature_quotes.text: The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.
signature_quotes.context: From his 1889 essay 'The Gospel of Wealth', articulating his view on the proper administration of wealth.

signature_quotes.text: To try to make the world in some way better than you found it is to have a noble motive in life.
Labor, capital, and ability are a three-legged stool... They are equal members of the great triple alliance which moves the industrial world.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the necessary components of industrial success.

signature_quotes.text: Whoso wants to share the heroism of battle, let him join the fight against ignorance and disease and the mad idea that war is necessary.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting his pacifist views and belief in fighting societal ills rather than other nations.

signature_quotes.text: The emigrant is the capable, energetic, ambitious, discontented man.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on his own immigrant journey and the nature of those who seek a new life.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It disgraces the individual and fails to utilize the wealth as a trust for the public good during the owner's lifetime.

anti_patterns.description: Binding philanthropic trustees to rigid, perpetual causes.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Earthly conditions inevitably change, making rigid mandates obsolete or ineffective over time.
It is a 'mad idea'; true heroism lies in fighting ignorance and disease, not engaging in international conflict.
